title: Cheese straws
subtitle: Shortcrust pastry straws with cheddar and cayenne
excerpt: Twenty-four shortcrust cheese straws made with 200 g of plain flour, 100 g of cold butter, and 100 g of mature cheddar, seasoned with cayenne and mustard. Ready in 35 minutes. Serve warm.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: cheese straws are documented in the section on savoury pastry biscuits, made from shortcrust or puff pastry trimmings enriched with grated cheese. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the savoury biscuits chapter documents cheese-flavoured shortcrust pastry rolled thin and cut into strips. The British domestic baking tradition of cheese straws using cheddar with mustard and cayenne is documented continuously from the Victorian period; the ingredients and method here are consistent with the public-domain literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked straws on the tray, then transfer to a bag. Bake from frozen, adding 3 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly; roll in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry dough keeps refrigerated for 2 days. Roll, cut, and bake when needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of working cold fat into flour until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Use your fingertips and lift the mixture as you rub, which aerates it. Work quickly so the butter stays cold. The flour-coated fat pieces form the structure of the shortcrust; if the fat melts, the pastry will be tough rather than short.

content.text: Cheese straws are the most forgiving pastry on this list. The shortcrust dough comes together quickly: flour rubbed with cold butter, enriched with grated cheddar and sharpened with cayenne and English mustard powder. It chills briefly, rolls thin, cuts into strips, and bakes in 12 minutes. They should be eaten warm, within an hour of baking.

items.prepNote: about ½ teaspoon — or more to taste
a pinch — the cheese is already salty
beaten with 2 tablespoons of cold water

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{english-mustard-powder}}, {{cayenne}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter dice and

until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{cheddar}} grated cheddar. Add the {{egg-yolks}} egg yolk mixture a tablespoon at a time, pressing the dough together after each addition, until it just comes together in a shaggy ball. Press into a disc, wrap, and chill for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with paper. Roll the chilled dough on a lightly floured surface to about 5 mm thick. Cut into strips about 1.5 cm wide and 12 cm long — 24 strips. Lay on the lined trays with a small gap between each. Twist each straw three or four times along its length if you want the spiral appearance.

Bake for 10–12 minutes, until the straws are golden and feel dry and firm when pressed — they will crisp further as they cool on the tray. Move to a rack after 2 minutes. Serve warm, within an hour of baking; the cheese flavour is most pronounced when they're still warm.
